summary:
The Hundred Days Reform was an ambitious but ultimately failed attempt to modernize the [[event:2337|Qing dynasty's]] governmental, educational, and military institutions through a series of decrees issued by the Guangxu Emperor between June and September 1898. Inspired by Japan's successful [[event:2544|Meiji Restoration]] and driven by the reformist scholars Kang Youwei and Liang Qichao, the reform program sought to transform China into a constitutional monarchy capable of competing with the Western powers.

The reform movement gained imperial sponsorship following the trauma of the [[event:2550|First Sino-Japanese War]] defeat in 1895 and the subsequent [[event:2586|scramble for concessions]] that threatened China's territorial integrity. The Guangxu Emperor, sympathetic to modernization, issued over 100 edicts in 103 days (11 June to 21 September 1898) calling for the modernization of the civil service examination system, the establishment of new schools teaching Western sciences, military modernization on Japanese lines, and reforms of the bureaucracy and legal system.

The reform program was cut short by a conservative coup orchestrated by the Empress Dowager Cixi, who placed the Guangxu Emperor under house arrest on 21 September 1898 and rescinded most of the reform decrees. Several of the leading reformers were executed; Kang Youwei and Liang Qichao escaped abroad. The conservative backlash they represented fueled the xenophobic Boxer movement and delayed Chinese political modernization — the very outcome the reformers had sought to prevent. The failure of the Hundred Days Reform demonstrated the limits of reform from above when confronted by entrenched conservative interests at the Qing court.
